title: Expressions and Equations
blurb: Write and evaluate expressions with exponents and variables, build equivalent expressions, and solve one-step equations.

lessons.title: Exponents
lessons.concreteComparison: A rumor that doubles every hour: $1$ person tells $2$, who tell $4$, then $8, 16, 32, \ldots$ After $5$ hours that's $2^5 = 32$ people. An **exponent** is shorthand for that repeated multiplication.
lessons.objective: Write repeated multiplication using exponents and evaluate whole-number powers, naming the base and exponent.
concept: An **exponent** tells how many times to multiply the **base** by itself: $2^3 = 2\times2\times2 = 8$.
The **base** is the number being multiplied; the small raised **exponent** is the count.
lessons.keyIdea: $b^n$ means multiply $b$ by itself $n$ times. The base is multiplied; the exponent counts.

facts.title: Powers of 2
facts.explanation: Each is the one before times 2.

workedExamples.statement: Write $3\times3\times3\times3$ with an exponent and find its value.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying the base by the exponent.

commonMistakes.correction: $7^1 = 7$ — the base is used just once.

steps.title: A power is repeated multiplication
steps.teach: Change the **base** and the **exponent** and watch the expansion. The exponent tells you how many copies of the base to multiply together.

steps.title: Base and exponent
beats: In $2^3$, the **base** is $2$ and the **exponent** is $3$.

examples.prompt: Write $3\times3\times3\times3$ as a power and evaluate.

steps.title: Evaluate the power
steps.intro: Multiply the base that many times.

steps.title: A common trap
beats: The exponent means **repeated multiplication**, not one multiply.

steps.title: The doubling chessboard
steps.body: Put $1$ grain of rice on the first chessboard square and double it each square: $1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots$ By the $64$th square you'd owe $2^{63}$ grains — more rice than has ever been grown on Earth. That's how fast exponents grow.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $b^n$ means multiply the **base** $b$ by itself $n$ times.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review powers with quick evaluations, a name check, and the classic exponent trap.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Exponents"
steps.body: Great start — you can read powers, expand them, and dodge the $2^3\neq2\times3$ trap. Next: the Order of Operations, where exponents take their place in line.

lessons.title: Order of Operations
lessons.concreteComparison: Follow a recipe out of order — bake before you mix — and dinner is ruined. Math expressions need an agreed order too, so that $3 + 4\times2$ means the same thing to everyone. The rule (PEMDAS) makes it exactly $11$.
lessons.objective: Evaluate numerical expressions using the order of operations: grouping, exponents, then ×/÷ left to right, then +/− left to right.
concept: Without a rule, $3 + 4\times2$ could be $14$ or $11$. The agreed order makes it **11**.
Order: **P**arentheses, **E**xponents, **M**ultiply/**D**ivide (left→right), **A**dd/**S**ubtract (left→right).
Multiply and divide rank **equal** — do them left to right; the same goes for add and subtract.
lessons.keyIdea: PEMDAS: parentheses, exponents, then ×/÷ left-to-right, then +/− left-to-right.

commonMistakes.correction: $3 + 4\times2$: the multiply comes first, giving $11$, not $14$.

steps.title: One operation at a time
steps.teach: Tap **Next step** to evaluate $3 + 4\times2$ in the right order. The multiply has higher priority, so it happens before the add.

steps.title: Why we need an order
beats: Mathematicians agreed on **one** order so answers match.

steps.title: Which comes first?
steps.intro: Don't just go left to right.

steps.title: Same rank, go left to right
beats: Multiply and divide are **equal** in rank.
Add and subtract work the same way.

steps.title: Mind the order
steps.intro: Parentheses, then powers, then the rest.

steps.title: The viral math fights
steps.body: Those “$6 \div 2(1+2) = ?$” posts that start internet arguments aren't really about math — they exploit sloppy notation. With clear parentheses and PEMDAS, every well-written expression has exactly **one** correct value.

steps.title: What you learned
points: × and ÷ rank equal — left to right; so do + and −.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review the order of operations with multiplies, parentheses, a power, and a match-the-value puzzle.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Order of Operations"
steps.body: Well done — PEMDAS is now second nature. Next we bring in letters: Variables & Expressions, where a symbol stands in for a number.

lessons.title: Variables & Expressions
lessons.concreteComparison: A taxi charges $\$3$ to start plus $\$2$ a mile. For $m$ miles the fare is $2m + 3$ dollars. A **variable** is a letter that stands in for a number — one you don't know yet, or one that can change.
lessons.objective: Write algebraic expressions from words and identify variables, coefficients, constants, and terms.
concept: A **variable** is a letter standing for an unknown or changing number, like $n$, $x$, or $m$.
A **coefficient** multiplies a variable: in $2m$, the coefficient is $2$ (and $2m$ means $2\times m$).
A **constant** is a plain number; a **term** is a part separated by $+$ or $-$. $2m + 3$ has terms $2m$ and $3$.
Words become expressions: “$3$ more than $x$” $= x + 3$; “$5$ times $y$” $= 5y$.
lessons.keyIdea: A variable stands for a number. $2m$ means $2\times m$; an expression like $2m+3$ joins terms with + or −.

workedExamples.statement: Write “seven more than a number $n$” as an expression.
What is the coefficient of $x$ in $4x + 5$?

commonMistakes.correction: $2m$ means $2\times m$ — two times the value of $m$.
In $3x + 5$, the **coefficient** is $3$ (it multiplies $x$); the **constant** is $5$ (it stands alone).

tryIt.statement: Write “twice a number $y$” as an expression.

steps.title: Tiles for an expression
steps.teach: Each accent tile is one $x$ — an unknown amount — and each small square is $1$. Build $2x + 3$ and see what the expression's pieces mean.

steps.title: What a variable means
beats: A **variable** is a letter for a number we don't fix yet.
$2m$ means $2\times m$ — the coefficient $2$ multiplies it.

steps.title: Coefficient, constant, term
beats: Terms are the building blocks of an expression.

steps.title: Words into expressions
steps.intro: Pick the operation the words describe.

steps.title: Spotting the pieces
beats: Naming the pieces helps you work with them.

problems.prompt: What is the coefficient of $y$ in $8y + 1$?

steps.title: Why letters?
steps.body: The habit of using letters for unknowns took off in the 1500s, and René Descartes set the style we still use: letters near the **start** of the alphabet ($a, b, c$) for fixed numbers and letters near the **end** ($x, y, z$) for unknowns. That's why “solve for $x$” sounds so natural.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **variable** is a letter standing for a number; $2m$ means $2\times m$.
**Coefficient** multiplies the variable; **constant** stands alone; **terms** split at + and −.
Words map to operations: “more than” $\to +$, “times” $\to \times$, “less than” $\to -$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review variables with word-to-expression translations and a few name-the-part questions.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: What is the coefficient of $x$ in $9x + 4$?

steps.title: You've finished "Variables & Expressions"
steps.body: Nice — you can turn words into expressions and name every part. Next: Evaluating Expressions, where you finally plug in a number and compute.

lessons.title: Evaluating Expressions
lessons.concreteComparison: The taxi fare $2m + 3$ becomes a real price once you know the miles: for $4$ miles it's $2(4) + 3 = \$11$. **Evaluating** an expression means substituting the value and doing the arithmetic.
lessons.objective: Evaluate algebraic expressions by substituting given values and applying the order of operations.
concept: **Evaluate** = replace the variable with its value, then compute.
A number next to a variable means multiply, so substitute and then follow PEMDAS.
lessons.keyIdea: To evaluate, substitute the value for the variable, then follow the order of operations.

commonMistakes.text: Sticking the digits together: $3x$ at $x=4$ giving $34$.
Skipping order of operations after substituting.
commonMistakes.correction: $2 + 3x$ at $x=4$ is $2 + 12 = 14$ — multiply before you add.

steps.title: Plug in and compute
steps.teach: Drag the slider to pick a value for $x$. The expression $3x + 2$ substitutes it and shows the result. Substituting is just replace-then-calculate.

steps.title: The hidden times sign
beats: Then finish with order of operations.

steps.title: Finish the calculation
steps.intro: Mind the order after you substitute.

steps.title: Spreadsheets evaluate too
steps.body: Every formula cell in a spreadsheet is an expression with variables — the other cells. Type $=3\ast A1+2$ and change $A1$, and the cell re-evaluates instantly. You've been doing algebra evaluation whenever you've used one.

steps.title: What you learned
points: After substituting, follow the **order of operations**.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review evaluating with simple substitutions, a power, and an expression with parentheses.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Evaluating Expressions"
steps.body: Great — you can substitute and compute with order of operations in play. Next: Equivalent Expressions, where we rewrite without changing the value.

lessons.title: Equivalent Expressions
lessons.concreteComparison: $x + x + x$ and $3x$ describe the same amount — like saying “three quarters” or “$75$ cents.” **Equivalent expressions** have the same value for every value of the variable. Combining like terms and the distributive property are how we make them.
lessons.objective: Generate equivalent expressions by combining like terms and applying the distributive property.
concept: **Like terms** have the same variable part. $2x$ and $3x$ are like; $2x$ and $3$ are not.
**Combine like terms** by adding their coefficients: $2x + 3x = 5x$.
**Distributive property**: $a(b + c) = ab + ac$. So $2(x + 3) = 2x + 6$.
Equivalent expressions are equal for **every** value of the variable.
lessons.keyIdea: Combine like terms ($2x+3x=5x$) and distribute ($2(x+3)=2x+6$) to rewrite an expression without changing its value.

commonMistakes.correction: $2x + 3$ stays $2x + 3$ — you can't add a variable term to a constant.

commonMistakes.text: Distributing to only the first term.

steps.title: Collect the like terms
steps.teach: The tiles start scattered. Tap **Collect like terms** to group the $x$-tiles together and the units together — that's exactly what $3x + 4$ means, and why $x + 1 + x + x + 1 + 1 + 1$ equals it.

steps.title: Like terms combine
beats: $2x$ and $3$ are **not** like — leave them apart.

steps.title: The distributive property
beats: Multiply the outside number by **each** inside term.
It works because $2$ groups of $(x+3)$ is $2x$ and $6$.

steps.title: Combining like terms
steps.intro: Add the coefficients of matching terms.

steps.title: Expanding with the distributive property
beats: The expanded form is equivalent — same value for every $x$.

steps.title: Expanding
steps.intro: Multiply the outside by each inside term.

steps.title: Same expression, many disguises
steps.body: $2(x + 3)$, $2x + 6$, and $x + x + 6$ all look different but are the **same** expression — they agree at $x=0, 1, 2,$ and every number in between. Algebra is largely the art of rewriting something into the disguise that's easiest to use.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Like terms** share a variable and combine by adding coefficients: $2x+3x=5x$.
**Equivalent** expressions match for every value of the variable.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review with combining like terms, expanding, and spotting which terms are actually alike.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Equivalent Expressions"
steps.body: Excellent — you can combine like terms and distribute to rewrite expressions. Last in this unit: One-Step Equations, where we finally solve for the unknown.

lessons.title: One-Step Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: Picture a balance scale. If $x + 3 = 7$, the two sides weigh the same. Take $3$ off **both** pans and it stays level: $x = 4$. **Solving** an equation means undoing operations until the variable is alone.
lessons.objective: Solve one-step equations using inverse operations (x+p=q, x−p=q, px=q, x÷p=q).
concept: An **equation** says two expressions are equal: $x + 3 = 7$.
**Solve** = find the variable's value by **undoing** with the inverse operation.
Whatever you do to one side, do to the **other** — the balance must stay level.
$x + 3 = 7 \to$ subtract 3 $\to x = 4$. $4x = 20 \to$ divide by 4 $\to x = 5$.
lessons.keyIdea: Undo the operation on the variable, doing the same to both sides. Add↔subtract, multiply↔divide.

commonMistakes.correction: To keep the balance, do the same operation to **both** sides.

commonMistakes.text: Using the same operation instead of the inverse.
commonMistakes.correction: For $x + 3 = 7$ you **subtract** 3, not add — undo with the opposite.

steps.title: Keep the balance
steps.teach: The scale shows $x + 3 = 7$. Tap **Subtract 3 from both sides** to take 3 weights off each pan — the balance holds and the $x$-box is left equal to $4$.

steps.title: Both sides, every time
beats: Change one side only and it tips — no longer equal.
So do the same to **both** sides.
That keeps the two sides equal.

steps.title: Split into equal groups
steps.teach: This scale shows $4x = 20$. Tap **Divide both sides by 4** to split each pan into 4 equal groups — one $x$-box balances $5$ weights, so $x = 5$.

steps.title: The first equals sign
steps.body: The symbol “$=$” was invented in 1557 by Welsh mathematician Robert Recorde, who picked two parallel lines because “no two things can be more equal.” Before that, people wrote “is equal to” out in full — every single time.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Solve** by undoing with the inverse, doing the same to **both** sides.
Add↔subtract and multiply↔divide are the inverse pairs.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's review one-step equations across add, subtract, multiply, and divide — plus the inverse-operation idea.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: To solve $x + 7 = 10$, what do you do to both sides?

steps.title: You've finished "One-Step Equations"
steps.body: Fantastic — you can solve add, subtract, multiply, and divide equations by keeping the balance. That completes Expressions & Equations!
